nativeRange.habitat: No wild population — entirely an artificial aquaculture hybrid developed in Malaysia and Taiwan. Parent species occupy rocky shorelines, river margins, and lake edges in Central America.

decorPreferences: large smooth rocks or ceramic hides for territory
bare bottom or coarse gravel (avoids substrate-digging damage to hump)
minimal or no live planting — will uproot
canister or sump filtration rated 4–6× turnover for heavy bioload

diet.notes: Accepts high-protein pellets (dedicated Flowerhorn pellets, cichlid sticks), frozen/live foods (earthworms, krill, bloodworms, feeder shrimp), and occasional blanched vegetables. Color-enhancing foods containing astaxanthin or spirulina are widely used to intensify red and orange pigmentation. Avoid overfeeding — heavy bioload strains filtration. Feed 1–2× daily; remove uneaten food promptly.

goodWith: large armored plecos (Pterygoplichthys species) — with caution
large robust cichlids of similar aggression in very large tanks (400 L+) — with close monitoring
Polypterus species in sufficiently large setups — with caution
avoidWith: tetras, barbs, rasboras — will be killed
other Flowerhorns unless a proven breeding pair with a divider available
fish with long trailing fins (severe fin damage risk)

imageCandidates.notes: Adult male Flowerhorn with well-developed nuchal hump (kok) and vivid red-orange coloration. Clean background, good lateral profile shot.
FishBase photo gallery for hybrid flowerhorn entries — image licensing varies. Check individual contributor terms.

summary: Entirely artificial hybrid cichlid from Malaysian/Taiwanese aquaculture (1990s–2000s), bred from Amphilophus citrinellus, A. trimaculatus, and Vieja synspilum. Famous for its nuchal hump (‘kok’), vivid coloration, and trade strains (Kamfa, ZZ, Red Texas, Golden Monkey). Highly aggressive; best kept solo in a 380 L+ dedicated tank.
careNotes:
The Flowerhorn Cichlid is an entirely man-made hybrid with no natural range, originating from selective breeding programs in Malaysia and Taiwan during the late 1990s and early 2000s. Primary parental stock is generally accepted to include Amphilophus citrinellus (Midas Cichlid), A. trimaculatus (Three-spot Cichlid), and Vieja synspilum (Redhead Cichlid), though exact lineages vary by strain and are rarely documented. The defining trait is the cranial nuchal hump (‘kok’), which is more pronounced in males and in strains selected for hump size; hump development varies with genetics, diet, and hormonal state.

Tank requirements: A single adult requires a minimum of 380 L (100 US gal) with a footprint of at least 150 cm. Filtration must handle an exceptionally heavy bioload — a canister or sump rated at 4–6× tank volume per hour is the minimum. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are strongly recommended. Bare-bottom or coarse-gravel setups simplify cleaning and prevent substrate fouling.

Solitary keeping: Flowerhorns are best kept one per tank as an aggressive display fish. Conspecific aggression is extreme — two Flowerhorns housed together without a solid divider will frequently fight to the death. Breeding pairs require a divider panel that can be removed briefly under supervision. Some keepers attempt mixed large-cichlid tanks in very large (600 L+) aquaria, but this demands careful monitoring and a backup plan.

Trade strains: The hobby recognises dozens of named strains marketed under trade names — Kamfa (round body, white/yellow eyes, fan tail), ZZ (Zhen Zhu — pearlescent scales, protruding eyes), Red Texas (hybrid with Herichthys cyanoguttatus, vivid red body with blue spots), Golden Monkey (rare, considered premium), SRD (Super Red Dragon), and Bonsai (dwarf-ish, compact body). Coloration and hump expression differ substantially between strains.

Hybrid identity note: fw-fish-348 is recorded as a duplicate Flowerhorn Cichlid entry sharing the same hybrid scientific name. These two entries are merge candidates; fw-fish-349 is the canonical record. Any data from fw-fish-348 should be reconciled into this file and fw-fish-348 retired.

Health: Flowerhorns are prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), ich, and hexamita (hole-in-the-head disease) under poor water quality. Maintain temperature 26–30°C and avoid large swings. The kok may temporarily shrink during illness or stress — this is a reliable early health indicator.
breedingNotes: Flowerhorns are fertile hybrids — unlike many fish hybrids, they can reproduce. Sexing: males typically develop a larger kok, more vivid coloration, and a pointed genital papilla; females have a rounded papilla and are generally smaller. Conditioning: raise temperature to 28–30°C and increase protein-rich live/frozen foods. A divider panel between the pair is strongly recommended during introductions — remove briefly to allow spawning when both fish display side-by-side without aggression. Spawning occurs on flat smooth surfaces (slate, smooth rocks, bare tank bottom). Clutch size ranges from 500–2,000+ eggs. Both parents guard eggs and fry vigorously; parental aggression toward the keeper increases during this period. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 28°C; fry become free-swimming within 5–7 days and accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro-pellets. Remove one parent if aggression between the pair escalates during fry care. Fry from Flowerhorn pairs are Flowerhorns (phenotypic variation across the brood is normal given the complex hybrid lineage).

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Flowerhorn Cichlid Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade-hobby source. Covers tank size (100 gal minimum), temperature (78–86°F / 26–30°C), pH (7.0–8.0), diet (high-protein pellets, frozen/live foods), aggressive solitary temperament, breeding basics, and trade strain overview. Tank minimum 380 L adopted from their 100-gal recommendation.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Flowerhorn Cichlid
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (pH 7.0–8.0, temperature 26–30°C, hardness 8–20 dGH), adult size (~30 cm), hybrid parentage (A. citrinellus × A. trimaculatus × Vieja synspilum lineage), aggressive solitary behavior, and breeding notes. Confirmed no wild range.

additional.name: FishBase — Amphilophus citrinellus (Midas Cichlid, primary parent)
additional.notes: Used to verify parental species taxonomy (family Cichlidae, order Cichliformes), native range (Central America — Nicaragua/Costa Rica), and baseline water parameters for the primary parent contributing to Flowerhorn lineage. Confirms A. citrinellus nuchal hump development in wild fish.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Flowerhorn Cichlid
additional.notes: Confirmed hybrid origin history (Malaysia/Taiwan late 1990s), kok development, common strain descriptions (Kamfa, Zhen Zhu, Red Texas, Golden Monkey), solitary aggressive temperament, minimum tank 400 L for adults, diet recommendations. Breeding notes on divider-panel introduction method.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Flowerhorn: Asia’s Most Controversial Cichlid
additional.notes: Historical context on Flowerhorn development and cultural significance in Southeast Asia, strain proliferation, and welfare concerns around artificially enhanced hump specimens. Confirmed multi-species hybrid ancestry. Cross-checked lifespan (8–12 years in optimal conditions) and adult size range (25–35 cm depending on strain).

fish.breedingNotes: Fertile hybrids capable of spawning. Divider-panel introduction method strongly recommended; both parents guard clutch of 500–2,000+ eggs. Fry free-swimming within 5–7 days post-hatch. Moderate rating due to conspecific aggression management requirement during pair introduction.
